shortDescription: Brain tumor surgery in Korea uses image-guided microsurgery and awake mapping at Seoul's accredited neurosurgery centers.
quickAnswer: Brain tumor surgery removes or reduces an abnormal mass in the brain, typically through a **craniotomy** performed by a specialist neurosurgical team.
Leading Korean centers in **Seoul and Gangnam** combine microsurgery with **intraoperative MRI, neuronavigation, and awake brain mapping** to protect speech and movement.
Approximate self-pay cost in Korea is **around US$18,000–US$45,000**, varying widely by tumor type, size, location, and complexity.
Plan to stay in Korea **roughly 3–6 weeks** for assessment, surgery, monitoring, and early follow-up before clearance to travel.

keyFacts.label: Procedure time
keyFacts.value: Approx. 3–7 hours, depending on tumor size and location

keyFacts.label: Anesthesia
keyFacts.value: General anesthesia; selected cases use awake-asleep-awake technique

keyFacts.label: Hospital stay
keyFacts.value: Around 5–10 days, often with 1–2 days in intensive care

keyFacts.label: Recommended stay in Korea
keyFacts.value: Approx. 3–6 weeks for surgery and early follow-up

keyFacts.label: Recovery
keyFacts.value: Light activity at 4–6 weeks; fuller recovery over 3–6 months

sections.overview:
Brain tumor surgery aims to remove as much of an abnormal mass as is safely possible while preserving healthy brain function. The most common approach is a **craniotomy**, in which a section of skull is temporarily opened so the neurosurgeon can access the tumor with microsurgical tools.

In Korea, large hospitals in **Seoul and the Gangnam district** run dedicated neurosurgery and neuro-oncology programs. These teams typically combine a neurosurgeon, neuro-oncologist, neuroradiologist, anesthesiologist, and rehabilitation specialists.

Korean centers often use **neuronavigation, intraoperative MRI, and functional brain mapping** to localize the tumor precisely and avoid critical areas controlling speech and movement.

Not every brain tumor needs immediate surgery. The plan depends on whether the tumor is benign or malignant, its size and location, and your overall health. Surgery may be combined with radiotherapy or chemotherapy in a multidisciplinary plan.
sections.candidacy:
Whether brain tumor surgery is appropriate depends on detailed imaging and a specialist neurosurgical assessment. Surgery is generally considered when a tumor is accessible and removal is expected to help.

You may be a candidate if:

- Imaging (MRI/CT) shows a tumor that is **surgically reachable** with acceptable risk
- The tumor causes symptoms such as headaches, seizures, vision changes, or weakness
- A **tissue diagnosis (biopsy)** is needed to guide further treatment
- Reducing tumor bulk could relieve pressure or improve other therapies

Surgery may be less suitable or need extra planning when:

- The tumor sits in or near critical functional or deep brain regions
- There are multiple lesions or widespread disease
- General anesthesia carries high risk due to other health conditions

A responsible Korean team will review your records, may request updated scans, and discuss realistic goals. Some patients are offered **awake craniotomy** so neurological function can be tested during surgery.
sections.procedure_steps:
Brain tumor surgery is highly individualized, but the pathway in a Korean neurosurgery center generally follows these stages.

- **Assessment:** Updated MRI or CT, blood work, and consultations with neurosurgery and anesthesiology to confirm the plan.
- **Planning:** The team maps the tumor using neuronavigation and, where relevant, prepares for **intraoperative monitoring**.
- **Anesthesia:** Most cases use general anesthesia. For tumors near speech or motor areas, an **awake-asleep-awake** technique may be used so function can be tested.
- **Craniotomy:** A section of skull is temporarily removed to expose the tumor under the operating microscope.
- **Resection:** The neurosurgeon removes as much tumor as is safely possible, guided by imaging and mapping.
- **Closure:** The skull section is replaced and secured, and the scalp is closed.

Removed tissue is sent for pathology to confirm the tumor type, which guides any further treatment.
sections.recovery_timeline:
Recovery after brain tumor surgery is gradual and varies with tumor type, location, and your overall health. The phases below are general guidance, not a guarantee.

- **First 1–2 days:** Often monitored in intensive care, with frequent neurological checks to watch for swelling or bleeding.
- **Days 3–10:** Continued hospital recovery; postoperative swelling tends to peak around day three. Walking and basic activity are encouraged as tolerated.
- **Weeks 1–6:** Recovery at home or accommodation; many people return to **light daily activity around 4–6 weeks**.
- **Months 3–6:** Fuller functional recovery, including possible return to work or exercise, depending on your case.

Some patients need **physical, occupational, or speech therapy** to regain strength, coordination, or language. Your Korean team will set follow-up scans and explain warning signs that require urgent attention. Always follow your surgeon's specific instructions before traveling home.
sections.cost_breakdown:
Brain tumor surgery costs in Korea vary considerably because every case differs in tumor type, size, location, and complexity. The figures below are **approximate self-pay estimates in US dollars** and should be confirmed with a formal hospital quote.

A representative package may fall **around US$18,000–US$45,000**, often lower than comparable surgery in the United States, where complex brain tumor care can run much higher.

Korean hospitals frequently require a **deposit (commonly around 10%)** to confirm a surgical date. Quotes may or may not include imaging, pathology, intensive care, medications, and rehabilitation, so ask exactly what is covered.

Additional costs to budget for include:

- Flights and local transport
- Accommodation for an extended stay
- A companion or caregiver
- Interpreter or coordinator fees, if not bundled

Always request an itemized estimate in writing before committing.
sections.why_korea:
Korea has become one of Asia's leading destinations for advanced medical care, with foreign patient numbers reaching record highs in recent years. For complex neurosurgery, several factors stand out.

- **Concentrated expertise:** Major teaching hospitals in **Seoul and Gangnam** run high-volume neurosurgery and neuro-oncology programs.
- **Modern technology:** Centers commonly offer **intraoperative MRI, neuronavigation, and functional brain mapping**.
- **Regulatory oversight:** Medicines and devices are regulated by the **Ministry of Food and Drug Safety (MFDS)**, and several Seoul hospitals hold **Joint Commission International (JCI)** accreditation.
- **Government support:** **KHIDI** runs the official **Medical Korea** portal, which lists registered institutions for international patients.

Korea also requires registered international-patient institutions to carry malpractice liability coverage. For African patients, dedicated international offices can help coordinate scans, appointments, interpretation, and logistics across a longer treatment stay.

costTable.item: Craniotomy and tumor resection (surgeon and facility)

callouts.title: Bring your full imaging history
callouts.text: Carry original MRI and CT files, prior pathology, and a medication list. Up-to-date scans let the Korean neurosurgical team give a more accurate plan and quote before you travel.

keyTakeaways: Brain tumor surgery in Korea centers on image-guided craniotomy at accredited Seoul and Gangnam hospitals, often with intraoperative MRI and brain mapping.
Approximate self-pay costs of around US$18,000–US$45,000 vary widely by tumor type and complexity, so insist on an itemized written quote.
Plan an extended stay of roughly 3–6 weeks for surgery, monitoring, and early follow-up, and confirm rehabilitation and travel clearance with your surgeon.

faqs.q: Is brain tumor surgery in Korea safe?
faqs.a: Major Korean neurosurgery centers in Seoul and Gangnam use modern technology such as neuronavigation and intraoperative MRI, and several hold Joint Commission International (JCI) accreditation. Medicines and devices are regulated by the Ministry of Food and Drug Safety (MFDS). As with any brain surgery, individual risk depends on tumor type and location and should be discussed with your surgeon.

faqs.q: How much does brain tumor surgery cost in Korea?
faqs.a: Costs vary widely with tumor type, size, location, and complexity. A representative self-pay estimate is roughly US$18,000–US$45,000, often lower than comparable surgery in the United States. Always request an itemized written quote, since coverage of imaging, intensive care, and rehabilitation differs between hospitals.

faqs.q: How long should I stay in Korea for brain tumor surgery?
faqs.a: Most international patients plan to stay around 3–6 weeks. This allows time for assessment and updated imaging, the surgery itself, hospital recovery of roughly 5–10 days, and early follow-up before your surgeon clears you to fly home.

faqs.q: What is awake craniotomy and when is it used?
faqs.a: Awake craniotomy is a technique where part of the surgery is performed while you are awake so the team can test speech and movement in real time. It is considered for tumors near critical functional areas, helping the surgeon remove tumor tissue while protecting important brain functions.

faqs.q: Will I need treatment after surgery?
faqs.a: It depends on the pathology results. Some benign tumors may need only monitoring, while others may be followed by radiotherapy, chemotherapy, or both as part of a multidisciplinary plan. Your Korean neuro-oncology team will explain the recommended next steps based on your diagnosis.
